narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, your academic profile shows solid but not standout performance compared to peers who typically succeed on the bar exam. Your family comes from an upper-middle income background, and you're planning to attend law school full-time, which is the norm for about 93% of students in the dataset used for this prediction. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to forecast first-time bar exam outcomes, estimated a 41.9% probability that you would fail the bar—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass with confidence. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the prediction. Your LSAT score had the strongest pull, working strongly in your favor by signaling potential to pass, even though it's a bit below the average of 36.7. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA played a supportive role, nudging the prediction toward success since it's right around the typical 3.21 average. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—meaning you placed in the lower third of your classmates—had the next biggest impact, pushing the overall outlook toward failure risk because lower deciles often correlate with bar struggles in the model's analysis. These top three elements outweighed others like your family income or full-time status, tipping the balance against you despite the positives. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher decile rankings and slightly stronger test scores, your profile landed in a borderline zone where the model saw elevated risk. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially with your consistent efforts reflected in that GPA. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted study or extracurriculars to improve your decile, and reapplying next cycle with these enhancements—many students in similar spots have turned predictions around this way.
